revision.comment: Created by translating the page "[[:ja:Special:Redirect/revision/92468342|江原神社]]"
revision.text:
{{神社|名称=江原神社|所在地=朝鮮江原道春川郡春川邑|祭神=天照大神
明治天皇
国魂大神
素戔嗚尊|社格=国幣小社|創建=[[1918年]]（大正7年）|例祭=10月15日}}'''Kogen-jinja''' Shrine was a shrine located in Chuncheon-gun, Gangwon-do, Korea The enshrined deities were Amaterasu Omikami, Emperor Meiji, Kunitama Omikami, and Susanoo no Mikoto. Sosawo-son was added to Mt. Gozu in Chuncheon, including Gisangbong Peak, as it was one of the identified places of Soshimori of Silla. It was associated with imperialism and State Shinto rather than local belief in Shintoism.{{Cite journal|last=Nakajima|first=Michio|date=2010|title=Shinto Deities that Crossed the Sea: Japan's "Overseas Shrines," 1868 to 1945|url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/27822898|journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies|volume=37|issue=1|pages=21–46|issn=0304-1042|jstor=27822898}}{{Rp|30}}   During that time, the government forced people to visit shrines instead of visiting them voluntarily.{{Cite journal|last=Nakajima|first=Michio|date=2010|title=Shinto Deities that Crossed the Sea: Japan's "Overseas Shrines," 1868 to 1945|url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/27822898|journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies|volume=37|issue=1|pages=21–46|issn=0304-1042|jstor=27822898}}{{Rp|38}} Because of Japan's loss in World War II, the shrine was abolished on November 17, 1945. 内務省告示第264号 官報第5660号（昭和20年11月22日） 1頁 . == Sources == {{脚注ヘルプ}} == Reference material == * Masaaki Aono, "'Inland and South Korea' in Colonial Korea and Ebara Shrine" Included in [http://www.tousuishobou.com/kenkyusyo/4-88708-256-8.htm "The Historical Phase of Modern Japan] " (edited by Tetsuya Ohama, [[1999]], ISBN 4-88708-256-8 ) [[Category:Gion faith]] [[Category:Kokuhei Shōsha]] [[Category:1918 establishments]]

国魂大神
素戔嗚尊|rank=国幣小社|established=[[1918年]]（大正7年）|festival=10月15日}}'''Kogen-jinja''' Shrine was a shrine located in Chuncheon-gun, Gangwon-do, Korea The enshrined deities were [[Amaterasu|Amaterasu Omikami]], [[Emperor Meiji]], [[Kunitama Omikami]], and [[Susanoo|Susanoo no Mikoto]]. Sosawo-son was added to Mt. Gozu in Chuncheon, including Gisangbong Peak, as it was one of the identified places of Soshimori of Silla. It was associated with imperialism and State Shinto rather than local belief in Shintoism.{{Cite journal|last=Nakajima|first=Michio|date=2010|title=Shinto Deities that Crossed the Sea: Japan's "Overseas Shrines," 1868 to 1945|url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/27822898|journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies|volume=37|issue=1|pages=21–46|issn=0304-1042|jstor=27822898}}{{Rp|30}}   During that time, the government forced people to visit shrines instead of visiting them voluntarily.{{Cite journal|last=Nakajima|first=Michio|date=2010|title=Shinto Deities that Crossed the Sea: Japan's "Overseas Shrines," 1868 to 1945|url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/27822898|journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies|volume=37|issue=1|pages=21–46|issn=0304-1042|jstor=27822898}}{{Rp|38}} Because 明治天皇
国魂大神
素戔嗚尊|rank=国幣小社|established=[[1918年]]（大正7年）|festival=10月15日}}'''Kogen-jinja''' Shrine was a shrine located in Chuncheon-gun, Gangwon-do, Korea The enshrined deities were [[Amaterasu|Amaterasu Omikami]], [[Emperor Meiji]], [[Kunitama Omikami]], and [[Susanoo|Susanoo no Mikoto]]. Sosawo-son was added to Mt. Gozu in Chuncheon, including Gisangbong Peak, as it was one of the identified places of Soshimori of Silla. It was associated with imperialism and State Shinto rather than local belief in Shintoism.{{Cite journal|last=Nakajima|first=Michio|date=2010|title=Shinto Deities that Crossed the Sea: Japan's "Overseas Shrines," 1868 to 1945|url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/27822898|journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies|volume=37|issue=1|pages=21–46|issn=0304-1042|jstor=27822898}}{{Rp|30}}   During that time, the government forced people to visit shrines instead of visiting them voluntarily.{{Cite journal|last=Nakajima|first=Michio|date=2010|title=Shinto Deities that Crossed the Sea: Japan's "Overseas Shrines," 1868 to 1945|url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/27822898|journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies|volume=37|issue=1|pages=21–46|issn=0304-1042|jstor=27822898}}{{Rp|38}} Because of Japan's loss in World War II, the shrine was abolished on November 17, 1945.内務省告示第264号 官報第5660号（昭和20年11月22日） 1頁 == References == == Sources == * Masaaki Aono, "'Inland and South Korea' in Colonial Korea and Ebara Shrine" Included in [http://www.tousuishobou.com/kenkyusyo/4-88708-256-8.htm "The Historical Phase of Modern Japan] " (edited by Tetsuya Ohama, [[1999]], ISBN 4-88708-256-8 ) 素戔嗚尊|rank=国幣小社|established=[[1918年]]（大正7年）|festival=10月15日}}'''Kogen-jinja''' Shrine was a shrine located in Chuncheon-gun, Gangwon-do, Korea The enshrined deities were [[Amaterasu|Amaterasu Omikami]], [[Emperor Meiji]], [[Kunitama Omikami]], and [[Susanoo|Susanoo no Mikoto]]. Sosawo-son was added to Mt. Gozu in Chuncheon, including Gisangbong Peak, as it was one of the identified places of Soshimori of Silla. It was associated with imperialism and State Shinto rather than local belief in Shintoism.{{Cite journal|last=Nakajima|first=Michio|date=2010|title=Shinto Deities that Crossed the Sea: Japan's "Overseas Shrines," 1868 to 1945|url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/27822898|journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies|volume=37|issue=1|pages=21–46|issn=0304-1042|jstor=27822898}}{{Rp|30}}   During that time, the government forced people to visit shrines instead of visiting them voluntarily.{{Cite journal|last=Nakajima|first=Michio|date=2010|title=Shinto Deities that Crossed the Sea: Japan's "Overseas Shrines," 1868 to 1945|url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/27822898|journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies|volume=37|issue=1|pages=21–46|issn=0304-1042|jstor=27822898}}{{Rp|38}} Because of Japan's loss in World War II, the shrine was abolished on November 17, 1945.内務省告示第264号 官報第5660号（昭和20年11月22日） 1頁 == References == == Sources == * Masaaki Aono, "'Inland and South Korea' in Colonial Korea and Ebara Shrine" Included in [http://www.tousuishobou.com/kenkyusyo/4-88708-256-8.htm "The Historical Phase of Modern Japan] " (edited by Tetsuya Ohama, [[1999]], ISBN 4-88708-256-8 ) 国魂大神
素戔嗚尊|rank=国幣小社|established=[[1918年]]（大正7年）|festival=10月15日}}'''Kogen-jinja''' Shrine was a shrine located in Chuncheon-gun, Gangwon-do, Korea The enshrined deities were [[Amaterasu|Amaterasu Omikami]], [[Emperor Meiji]], [[Kunitama Omikami]], and [[Susanoo|Susanoo no Mikoto]]. Sosawo-son was added to Mt. Gozu in Chuncheon, including Gisangbong Peak, as it was one of the identified places of Soshimori of Silla. It was associated with imperialism and State Shinto rather than local belief in Shintoism.{{Cite journal|last=Nakajima|first=Michio|date=2010|title=Shinto Deities that Crossed the Sea: Japan's "Overseas Shrines," 1868 to 1945|url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/27822898|journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies|volume=37|issue=1|pages=21–46|issn=0304-1042|jstor=27822898}}{{Rp|30}}   During that time, the government forced people to visit shrines instead of visiting them voluntarily.{{Cite journal|last=Nakajima|first=Michio|date=2010|title=Shinto Deities that Crossed the Sea: Japan's "Overseas Shrines," 1868 to 1945|url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/27822898|journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies|volume=37|issue=1|pages=21–46|issn=0304-1042|jstor=27822898}}{{Rp|38}} Because of Japan's loss in World War II, the shrine was abolished on November 17, 1945.内務省告示第264号 官報第5660号（昭和20年11月22日） 1頁 == References == == Sources == * Masaaki Aono, "'Inland and South Korea' in Colonial Korea and Ebara Shrine" Included in [http://www.tousuishobou.com/kenkyusyo/4-88708-256-8.htm "The Historical Phase of Modern Japan] " (edited by Tetsuya Ohama, [[1999]], ISBN 4-88708-256-8 ) {{infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|name=江原神社|location=朝鮮江原道春川郡春川邑|deity=天照大神
明治天皇
国魂大神
素戔嗚尊|rank=国幣小社|established=[[1918]]（大正7年）|festival=10月15日}}'''Kogen-jinja''' Shrine was a shrine located in Chuncheon-gun, Gangwon-do, Korea The enshrined deities were [[Amaterasu|Amaterasu Omikami]], [[Emperor Meiji]], [[Kunitama Omikami]], and [[Susanoo|Susanoo no Mikoto]]. Sosawo-son was added to Mt. Gozu in Chuncheon, including Gisangbong Peak, as it was one of the identified places of Soshimori of Silla. It was associated with imperialism and State Shinto rather than local belief in Shintoism.{{Cite journal|last=Nakajima|first=Michio|date=2010|title=Shinto Deities that Crossed the Sea: Japan's "Overseas Shrines," 1868 to 1945|url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/27822898|journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies|volume=37|issue=1|pages=21–46|issn=0304-1042|jstor=27822898}}{{Rp|30}}   During that time, the government forced people to visit shrines instead of visiting them voluntarily.{{Cite journal|last=Nakajima|first=Michio|date=2010|title=Shinto Deities that Crossed the Sea: Japan's "Overseas Shrines," 1868 to 1945|url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/27822898|journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies|volume=37|issue=1|pages=21–46|issn=0304-1042|jstor=27822898}}{{Rp|38}} Because of Japan's loss in World War II, the shrine was abolished on November 17, 1945.内務省告示第264号 官報第5660号（昭和20年11月22日） 1頁 == References == == Sources == * Masaaki Aono, "'Inland and South Korea' in Colonial Korea and Ebara Shrine" Included in [http://www.tousuishobou.com/kenkyusyo/4-88708-256-8.htm "The Historical Phase of Modern Japan] " (edited by Tetsuya Ohama, [[1999]], ISBN 4-88708-256-8 ) revision.comment: Text replacement - "qq" to "11"
revision.text:
{{infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|name=江原神社|location=朝鮮江原道春川郡春川邑|deity=天照大神
明治天皇
国魂大神
素戔嗚尊|rank=国幣小社|established=[[1918]]（大正7年）|festival=10月15日}}'''Kogen-jinja''' Shrine was a shrine located in Chuncheon-gun, Gangwon-do, Korea The enshrined deities were [[Amaterasu|Amaterasu Omikami]], [[Emperor Meiji]], [[Kunitama Omikami]], and [[Susanoo|Susanoo no Mikoto]]. Sosawo-son was added to Mt. Gozu in Chuncheon, including Gisangbong Peak, as it was one of the identified places of Soshimori of Silla. It was associated with imperialism and State Shinto rather than local belief in Shintoism.{{Cite journal|last=Nakajima|first=Michio|date=2010|title=Shinto Deities that Crossed the Sea: Japan's "Overseas Shrines," 1868 to 1945|url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/27822898|journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies|volume=37|issue=1|pages=21–46|issn=0304-1042|jstor=27822898}}{{Rp|30}}   During that time, the government forced people to visit shrines instead of visiting them voluntarily.{{Cite journal|last=Nakajima|first=Michio|date=2010|title=Shinto Deities that Crossed the Sea: Japan's "Overseas Shrines," 1868 to 1945|url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/27822898|journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies|volume=37|issue=1|pages=21–46|issn=0304-1042|jstor=27822898}}{{Rp|38}} Because of Japan's loss in World War II, the shrine was abolished on November 17, 1945.内務省告示第264号 官報第5660号（昭和20年11月22日） 1頁 == References == == Sources == * Masaaki Aono, "'Inland and South Korea' in Colonial Korea and Ebara Shrine" Included in [http://www.tousuishobou.com/kenkyusyo/4-88708-256-8.htm "The Historical Phase of Modern Japan] " (edited by Tetsuya Ohama, [[1999]], ISBN 4-88708-256-8 ) that time, the government forced people to visit shrines instead of visiting them voluntarily.{{Cite journal|last=Nakajima|first=Michio|date=2010|title=Shinto Deities that Crossed the Sea: Japan's "Overseas Shrines," 1868 to 1945|url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/27822898|journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies|volume=37|issue=1|pages=21–46|issn=0304-1042|jstor=27822898}}{{Rp|38}} Because 素戔嗚尊|rank=国幣小社|established=[[1918]]（大正7年）|festival=10月15日}}'''Kogen-jinja''' Shrine was a shrine located in Chuncheon-gun, Gangwon-do, Korea The enshrined deities were [[Amaterasu|Amaterasu Omikami]], [[Emperor Meiji]], [[Kunitama Omikami]], and [[Susanoo|Susanoo no Mikoto]]. Sosawo-son was added to Mt. Gozu in Chuncheon, including Gisangbong Peak, as it was one of the identified places of Soshimori of Silla. It was associated with imperialism and State Shinto rather than local belief in Shintoism.{{Cite journal|last=Nakajima|first=Michio|date=2010|title=Shinto Deities that Crossed the Sea: Japan's "Overseas Shrines," 1868 to 1945|url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/27822898|journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies|volume=37|issue=1|pages=21–46|issn=0304-1042|jstor=27822898}}{{Rp|30}}   During that time, the government forced people to visit shrines instead of visiting them voluntarily.{{Cite journal|last=Nakajima|first=Michio|date=2010|title=Shinto Deities that Crossed the Sea: Japan's "Overseas Shrines," 1868 to 1945|url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/27822898|journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies|volume=37|issue=1|pages=21–46|issn=0304-1042|jstor=27822898}}{{Rp|38}} Because of Japan's loss in World War II, the shrine was abolished on November 17, 1945.内務省告示第264号 官報第5660号（昭和20年11月22日） 1頁 == References == == Sources == * Masaaki Aono, "'Inland and South Korea' in Colonial {{infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|name=江原神社|location=朝鮮江原道春川郡春川邑|deity=天照大神
明治天皇
国魂大神
素戔嗚尊|rank=国幣小社|established=[[1918]]（大正7年）|festival=10月15日}}'''Kogen-jinja''' Shrine was a shrine located in Chuncheon-gun, Gangwon-do, Korea The enshrined deities were [[Amaterasu|Amaterasu Omikami]], [[Emperor Meiji]], [[Kunitama Omikami]], and [[Susanoo|Susanoo no Mikoto]]. Sosawo-son was added to Mt. Gozu in Chuncheon, including Gisangbong Peak, as it was one of the identified places of Soshimori of Silla. It was associated with imperialism and State Shinto rather than local belief in Shintoism.{{Cite journal|last=Nakajima|first=Michio|date=2010|title=Shinto Deities that Crossed the Sea: Japan's "Overseas Shrines," 1868 to 1945|url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/27822898|journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies|volume=37|issue=1|pages=21–46|issn=0304-1042|jstor=27822898}}{{Rp|30}}   During that time, the government forced people to visit shrines instead of visiting them voluntarily.{{Cite journal|last=Nakajima|first=Michio|date=2010|title=Shinto Deities that Crossed the Sea: Japan's "Overseas Shrines," 1868 to 1945|url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/27822898|journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies|volume=37|issue=1|pages=21–46|issn=0304-1042|jstor=27822898}}{{Rp|38}} Because of Japan's loss in World War II, the shrine was abolished on November 17, 1945.内務省告示第264号 官報第5660号（昭和20年11月22日） 1頁 == References == == Sources == * Masaaki Aono, "'Inland and South Korea' in Colonial Korea and Ebara Shrine" Included in [http://www.tousuishobou.com/kenkyusyo/4-88708-256-8.htm "The Historical Phase of Modern Japan] " (edited by Tetsuya Ohama, [[1999]], ISBN 4-88708-256-8 ) {{infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|name=江原神社|location=朝鮮江原道春川郡春川邑|deity=天照大神
明治天皇
国魂大神
素戔嗚尊|rank=国幣小社|established=[[1918]]（大正7th year ）|festival=10月15日}}'''Kogen-jinja''' Shrine was a shrine located in Chuncheon-gun, Gangwon-do, Korea The enshrined deities were [[Amaterasu|Amaterasu Omikami]], [[Emperor Meiji]], [[Kunitama Omikami]], and [[Susanoo|Susanoo no Mikoto]]. Sosawo-son was added to Mt. Gozu in Chuncheon, including Gisangbong Peak, as it was one of the identified places of Soshimori of Silla. It was associated with imperialism and State Shinto rather than local belief in Shintoism.{{Cite journal|last=Nakajima|first=Michio|date=2010|title=Shinto Deities that Crossed the Sea: Japan's "Overseas Shrines," 1868 to 1945|url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/27822898|journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies|volume=37|issue=1|pages=21–46|issn=0304-1042|jstor=27822898}}{{Rp|30}}   During that time, the government forced people to visit shrines instead of visiting them voluntarily.{{Cite journal|last=Nakajima|first=Michio|date=2010|title=Shinto Deities that Crossed the Sea: Japan's "Overseas Shrines," 1868 to 1945|url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/27822898|journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies|volume=37|issue=1|pages=21–46|issn=0304-1042|jstor=27822898}}{{Rp|38}} Because of Japan's loss in World War II, the shrine was abolished on November 17, 1945.内務省告示第264号 官報第5660号（昭和20th year 11月22日） 1頁 == References == == Sources == * Masaaki Aono, "'Inland and South Korea' in Colonial Korea and Ebara Shrine" Included in [http://www.tousuishobou.com/kenkyusyo/4-88708-256-8.htm "The Historical Phase of Modern Japan] " (edited by Tetsuya Ohama, [[1999]], ISBN 4-88708-256-8 ) It was associated with imperialism and State Shinto rather than local belief in Shintoism.{{Cite journal|last=Nakajima|first=Michio|date=2010|title=Shinto Deities that Crossed the Sea: Japan's "Overseas Shrines," 1868 to 1945|url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/27822898|journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies|volume=37|issue=1|pages=21–46|issn=0304-1042|jstor=27822898}}{{Rp|30}}   During that time, the government forced people to visit shrines instead of visiting them voluntarily.{{Cite journal|last=Nakajima|first=Michio|date=2010|title=Shinto Deities that Crossed the Sea: Japan's "Overseas Shrines," 1868 to 1945|url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/27822898|journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies|volume=37|issue=1|pages=21–46|issn=0304-1042|jstor=27822898}}{{Rp|38}} Because of Japan's loss in World War II, the shrine was abolished on November 17, 1945.内務省告示第264号 官報第5660号（昭和20th year 11月22日） 1頁 == References == == Sources == * Masaaki Aono, "'Inland and South Korea' in Colonial Korea and Ebara Shrine" Included in [http://www.tousuishobou.com/kenkyusyo/4-88708-256-8.htm "The Historical Phase of Modern Japan] " (edited by Tetsuya Ohama, [[1999]], ISBN 4-88708-256-8 ) {{Beppyo Shrines}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 7]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:11 2]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Gion faith]] [[Category:Kokuhei Shōsha]] [[Category:1918 establishments]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in the Japanese colonial empire]] [[Category:Shinmei shrines]] [[Category:11 7]] [[Category:shinto shrines]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:Kōgen Shrine]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] }} [[sn:Kōgen Shrine]] [[en:Kōgen Shrine]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Kōgen Shrine}}

素戔嗚尊|Rank=国幣小社|established=[[:en:1918|1918]]（大正7th year ）|festival=10月15日}}'''Kogen-jinja''' Shrine was a shrine located in Chuncheon-gun, Gangwon-do, Korea The enshrined deities were [[Amaterasu|Amaterasu Omikami]], [[:en:Emperor Meiji|Emperor Meiji]], [[Kunitama Omikami]], and [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo no Mikoto]]. revision.comment: removed [[Category:No valid interwikis]]; added [[Category:Qq]][[simple:Gangwon Shrine]] using [[Help:Gadget-HotCat|HotCat]]
revision.text:
{{infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|name=江原神社|location=朝鮮江原道春川郡春川邑|deity=天照大神
明治天皇
国魂大神
素戔嗚尊|Rank=国幣小社|established=[[:en:1918|1918]]（大正7th year ）|festival=10月15日}}'''Kogen-jinja''' Shrine was a shrine located in Chuncheon-gun, Gangwon-do, Korea The enshrined deities were [[Amaterasu|Amaterasu Omikami]], [[:en:Emperor Meiji|Emperor Meiji]], [[Kunitama Omikami]], and [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo no Mikoto]]. Sosawo-son was added to Mt. Gozu in Chuncheon, including Gisangbong Peak, as it was one of the identified places of Soshimori of Silla. It was associated with imperialism and State Shinto rather than local belief in Shintoism.{{Cite journal|last=Nakajima|first=Michio|date=2010|title=Shinto Deities that Crossed the Sea: Japan's "Overseas Shrines," 1868 to 1945|url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/27822898|journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies|volume=37|issue=1|pages=21–46|issn=0304-1042|jstor=27822898}}{{Rp|30}}   During that time, the government forced people to visit shrines instead of visiting them voluntarily.{{Cite journal|last=Nakajima|first=Michio|date=2010|title=Shinto Deities that Crossed the Sea: Japan's "Overseas Shrines," 1868 to 1945|url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/27822898|journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies|volume=37|issue=1|pages=21–46|issn=0304-1042|jstor=27822898}}{{Rp|38}} Because of Japan's loss in World War II, the shrine was abolished on November 17, 1945.内務省告示第264号 官報第5660号（昭和20th year 11月22日） 1頁 == References == == Sources == * Masaaki Aono, "'Inland and South Korea' in Colonial Korea and Ebara Shrine" Included in [http://www.tousuishobou.com/kenkyusyo/4-88708-256-8.htm "The Historical Phase of Modern Japan] " (edited by Tetsuya Ohama, [[:en:1999|1999]], ISBN 4-88708-256-8 ) {{Beppyo Shrines}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Kokuhei Shōsha]] [[Category:1918 establishments]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in the Japanese colonial empire]] [[Category:Shinmei shrines]] [[Category:shinto shrines]] }} [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:Pages without valid translation templates or en interwikis]] [[Category:Pages without valid translation templates or en interwikis or ja interwikis]] [[en:Kōgen Shrine]] [[Category:Missing wikidata]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Qq]][[simple:Gangwon Shrine]]

明治天皇
国魂大神
素戔嗚尊|Rank=国幣小社|established={{ill|1918|en|1918|WD=Q2094|lt=1918|1=1918}}（大正7th year ）|festival=10月15日}}'''Kogen-jinja''' Shrine was a shrine located in Chuncheon-gun, Gangwon-do, Korea The enshrined deities were [[Amaterasu|Amaterasu Omikami]], {{ill|Emperor Meiji|en|Emperor Meiji|WD=Q174231|lt=Emperor Meiji|1=Emperor Meiji}}, [[Kunitama Omikami]], and [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo no Mikoto]]. Sosawo-son was added to Mt. Gozu in Chuncheon, including Gisangbong Peak, as it was one of the identified places of Soshimori of Silla. It was associated with imperialism and State Shinto rather than local belief in Shintoism.{{Cite journal|last=Nakajima|first=Michio|date=2010|title=Shinto Deities that Crossed the Sea: Japan's "Overseas Shrines," 1868 to 1945|url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/27822898|journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies|volume=37|issue=1|pages=21–46|issn=0304-1042|jstor=27822898}}{{Rp|30}}   During that time, the government forced people to visit shrines instead of visiting them voluntarily.{{Cite journal|last=Nakajima|first=Michio|date=2010|title=Shinto Deities that Crossed the Sea: Japan's "Overseas Shrines," 1868 to 1945|url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/27822898|journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies|volume=37|issue=1|pages=21–46|issn=0304-1042|jstor=27822898}}{{Rp|38}} Because of Japan's loss in World War II, the shrine was abolished on November 17, 1945.内務省告示第264号 官報第5660号（昭和20th year 11月22日） 1頁 == References == == Sources == * Masaaki Aono, "'Inland and South Korea' in Colonial Korea and Ebara Shrine" Included in [http://www.tousuishobou.com/kenkyusyo/4-88708-256-8.htm "The Historical Phase of Modern Japan] " (edited by Tetsuya Ohama, {{ill|1999|en|1999|WD=Q2091|lt=1999|1=1999}}, ISBN 4-88708-256-8 ) {{Beppyo Shrines}} [[en:Kōgen Shrine]] {{wikidata link|Q11550715}}[[ja:江原神社]][[ko:강원신사]][[zh:江原神社]] [[simple:Gangwon Shrine]] [[Category:1918 establishments]] [[Category:Kokuhei Shōsha]] [[Category:Pages without valid translation templates or en interwikis]] [[Category:Shinmei shrines]] [[Category:shinto shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in the Japanese colonial empire]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:pages]]
